Jesus has ups and downs, just like us, just like any human being.<|18.88|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus depends upon friends and family.<|3.06|> <|3.86|> Jesus feasts with others.<|5.98|> <|6.16|> He knows hunger and joy and delight and pain.<|10.08|> <|11.36|> In the course of his ministry, he begins to recognize poverty,<|17.54|> <|18.44|> poverty of body and poverty of spirit.<|21.12|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> it.<|0.38|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.96|> He depends and enjoys his friends.<|3.70|> <|4.36|> And he eventually dies.<|6.62|> <|6.84|> In all of these ways, it's the same flesh.<|10.22|> <|10.32|> And that's why John doesn't need to tell the story of Mary or of Joseph<|16.08|> <|16.08|> or of the baby or the shepherds or the strange visitors from the east.<|21.36|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> His point is something else, that it's the same flesh and blood.<|5.04|> <|5.78|> And to tell their stories at this moment for John<|8.42|> <|8.42|> would distract from us telling our own stories<|12.08|> <|12.08|> about the word becoming flesh in our own lives.<|17.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So the manger for John, the creche for John, is our own homes, our own homelessness even.<|9.84|> <|10.44|> The neighborhood where it happens is not in Palestine, but in our backyards, in the midst of our own flesh and blood.<|18.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So, may we become quiet enough, kind enough, desperate enough to hear and feel the Word becoming flesh and living among us now.<|18.78|><|endoftext|>
